diagrams.title: Elliptic Curve Visualization
diagrams.description: A 2D coordinate plane showing the elliptic curve y² = x³ + ax + b with sample points and the point at infinity
elements: X-axis and Y-axis with labeled coordinates
Curved line representing the elliptic curve (symmetric about x-axis)
Point P on the upper branch of the curve with coordinates (x₁, y₁)
Point Q on the upper branch with coordinates (x₂, y₂)
Point -P reflected below the x-axis at (x₁, -y₁)
Point at infinity (∞) indicated at the top of the curve
Generator point G marked on the curve
Dotted vertical line through P showing the reflection to -P
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the geometric representation of elliptic curves and how points are reflected across the x-axis, which is fundamental to understanding the group operation in ECDSA

diagrams.title: ECDSA Key Generation Process
diagrams.description: Block diagram showing the steps to generate ECDSA key pair from curve parameters
elements: Input box: Curve parameters (a, b, p, G, n) where p is prime, G is generator point, n is order of G
Arrow pointing to random number generator
Box: Select random private key d (1 < d < n-1)
Arrow pointing to elliptic curve point multiplication module
Box: Compute public key Q = d × G (point multiplication)
Arrow pointing to output boxes: Private Key (d) and Public Key Q = (Qx, Qy)
Dotted box labeled 'Keep d secret' around private key
Dotted box labeled 'Share publicly' around public key
diagrams.purpose: Students visualize the complete key generation workflow and understand the relationship between private key (secret) and public key (shared), crucial for blockchain applications

diagrams.title: ECDSA Signature Generation Process
diagrams.description: Flowchart showing step-by-step signature creation with mathematical formulas
elements: Input box: Message m, Private key d
Arrow to Hash function box: Compute e = HASH(m)
Arrow to random generator: Choose random nonce k (1 < k < n-1)
Arrow to curve computation: Calculate (x₁, y₁) = k × G
Arrow to modulo operation: Compute r = x₁ mod n
Decision diamond: If r = 0, go back to choose new k
Arrow to s computation: s = k⁻¹ × (e + d × r) mod n
Decision diamond: If s = 0, go back to choose new k
Output box: Signature = (r, s)
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the complete signature generation algorithm including the critical importance of the random nonce k and its relationship to signature security

diagrams.title: ECDSA Signature Verification Process
diagrams.description: Flowchart showing signature verification steps with comparison logic
elements: Input boxes: Message m, Signature (r, s), Public key Q
Arrow to Hash function: Compute e = HASH(m)
Arrow to modular inverse: Compute w = s⁻¹ mod n
Split to two parallel computations:
Left branch: u₁ = e × w mod n, then u₁ × G = (x₁, y₁)
Right branch: u₂ = r × w mod n, then u₂ × Q = (x₂, y₂)
Arrow to point addition: v = (x₁ + x₂) mod n
Diamond decision: Check if v = r
Yes path to 'Signature VALID' box with checkmark
No path to 'Signature INVALID' box with X mark
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how verification works by recomputing r and comparing with original, reinforcing the mathematical foundation of ECDSA

diagrams.title: Point Addition Geometric Interpretation
diagrams.description: Geometric construction showing how two points on elliptic curve are added
elements: Elliptic curve with points P and Q marked
Line through P and Q extended to intersect curve at third point R'
Dotted line from R' reflected across x-axis to point R = P + Q
Labels: 'Draw line through P and Q', 'Find third intersection R'', 'Reflect R' across x-axis to get R'
Special case inset: Point P added to itself showing tangent line
diagrams.purpose: Students visualize the group operation (point addition) which is the foundation of elliptic curve cryptography, making the abstract math concrete

charts.title: DSA vs ECDSA vs RSA Comparison
charts.content:
Table comparing key sizes, security levels, performance, and applications:
- RSA: 2048-bit key, 112-bit security, slow signature, widely used
- DSA: 2048-bit key, 112-bit security, moderate speed, US government
- ECDSA: 256-bit key, 128-bit security, fast signatures, blockchain (Bitcoin, Ethereum)
Columns: Algorithm, Typical Key Size, Security Level (bits), Signature Speed, Primary Applications

charts.title: Complete ECDSA Lifecycle
charts.content:
Four-stage process flow:
1. Setup: Select elliptic curve parameters (secp256k1 for Bitcoin)
2. Key Generation: Generate private key d, compute public key Q = dG
3. Signing: Input message, hash, compute (r, s) using d and random k
4. Verification: Input message, signature, public key Q, compute and compare v with r
Arrows connecting each stage in circular flow

charts.title: ECDSA Mathematical Formulas Summary
charts.content:
Two-column table with operation and formula:
- Key Generation: Q = d × G
- Signature r: r = x₁ mod n (where kG = (x₁, y₁))
- Signature s: s = k⁻¹ × (H(m) + d × r) mod n
- Verification w: w = s⁻¹ mod n
- Verification u₁: u₁ = H(m) × w mod n
- Verification u₂: u₂ = r × w mod n
- Verification v: v = (x₁ + x₂) mod n

charts.title: ECDSA Security Considerations
charts.content:
Classification chart with three categories:
1. Nonce Security: Must be truly random, never reused, kept secret
2. Key Security: Private key must be protected, public key can be shared
3. Implementation Security: Constant-time operations, proper random number generation, side-channel attack prevention

branches: Mathematical Foundation: Elliptic curves (y² = x³ + ax + b), Point addition, Generator point G, Order n
Key Generation: Private key d (random), Public key Q = d×G (point multiplication), secp256k1 curve
Signature Components: r (x-coordinate of kG), s (computed using private key and nonce k), Hash H(m)
Security Basis: Discrete logarithm problem, Nonce importance, Collision resistance of hash
Applications: Bitcoin transactions, Ethereum, SSL/TLS certificates, Smart contract signatures
Verification: Compute w, u₁, u₂, Compare v = r mod n
mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview connecting mathematical foundations to practical applications in blockchain, helping students see how theory translates to real-world cryptographic systems

branches: Random Nonce k: Must be unique per signature, Never reveal k, k⁻¹ computation required
Private Key d: Keep absolutely secret, Used in s calculation, Compromised = signatures forgeable
Hash H(m): Provides message integrity, Must be collision-resistant, Different messages = different hashes
Public Key Q: Can be shared openly, Verifies signature authenticity, Derived from private key
Verification Check: Recompute r from computed points, Compare with original r value, Mathematical proof of ownership
mindMaps.purpose: Helps students understand the security implications of each component in ECDSA and why each element is critical for maintaining cryptographic security in blockchain systems
